skill.name: routeros-fundamentals
skill.description: RouterOS v7 knowledge for AI agents that need to write MikroTik CLI commands, REST calls, scripts, and configuration guidance.
skill.summary: Guide AI agents through MikroTik RouterOS v7 CLI, REST API, scripting, packages, users, and firewall workflows.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, credential, and filesystem patterns, but review shows they are primarily Markdown documentation and RouterOS examples, not executable skill code. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it teaches operational router administration, default credential handling, REST command execution, and device-mode changes.

medium_findings.title: Operational Router Administration Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents commands and REST calls that can reboot routers, fetch packages, run RouterOS actions, change device mode, and create privileged users. This is legitimate RouterOS guidance, but misuse could affect network availability or access control.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show operational router commands and REST actions. The surrounding files present them as administration references, not as hidden executable code.

medium_findings.title: Default Credentials and Plain HTTP Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill explains empty-password admin authentication and uses HTTP examples for RouterOS REST access. These are accurate defaults and lab patterns, but production users need stronger authentication and protected management access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk is clear from documented admin and HTTP examples, but the examples target RouterOS defaults rather than credential theft or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Hits Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The many shell, Ruby backtick, command substitution, and disk destruction detections come from Markdown examples or warnings about commands not to use. They are not executable installer scripts or hidden automation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose and fenced RouterOS syntax guidance. One flagged disk command appears in a warning that tells agents not to suggest it.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Access Hits Are Credential Handling Documentation
low_findings.description: The Keychain, environment variable, SSH key, and router file references describe safe handling and provisioning behavior. No evidence found of code that reads local secrets or sends them to an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documentation of credential source precedence and public key provisioning. The SSH private key path is described as a generated local artifact, not accessed by this skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and IP Addresses Are Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded HTTP URLs, localhost addresses, and MikroTik download endpoints are example values used to explain RouterOS REST and package workflows. No evidence found of covert external destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destinations are placeholders, localhost lab endpoints, or official MikroTik package URLs. They appear in reference text rather than executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: RouterOS REST Action Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes REST POST examples for router actions such as reboot, update checks, DNS cache flushing, and script execution. These actions require authorization and should be reviewed before use on production routers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples show authenticated RouterOS action calls. They are legitimate administrative examples but can change device state.

dangerous_patterns.title: RouterOS Device-Mode Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes enabling advanced device-mode features such as containers. This is intentional RouterOS administration, but it expands device capabilities and requires physical confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited text explicitly describes device-mode changes and required confirmation. The physical confirmation requirement reduces malicious automation risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Default Admin Authentication Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents empty password admin authentication for RouterOS defaults. This is accurate context, but users should avoid carrying this pattern into production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit and operational. They are not malicious, but they involve insecure defaults that need user awareness.

content.value_statement: RouterOS uses its own CLI, REST behavior, and scripting language, so generic Linux advice often fails. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code RouterOS-specific guidance for safer MikroTik administration.
actual_capabilities: Explains RouterOS v7 CLI paths and command syntax for common administration tasks.
Maps RouterOS REST verbs to create, update, delete, and command actions.
Documents user, SSH key, package, device-mode, firewall, and networking REST workflows.
Highlights RouterOS differences from GNU/Linux to prevent invalid shell advice.
Provides RouterOS version, package, architecture, and scripting reference details.
Identifies gotchas for long-running REST calls and Bun-based RouterOS tooling.
limitations: Covers RouterOS 7.x only; RouterOS 6 behavior may differ.
Does not connect to routers or verify live device state by itself.
Examples may need adaptation for production authentication, HTTPS, and network policy.
Some reference notes are documented as lab-tested, while others come from external documentation.

use_cases.title: RouterOS Command Planning
use_cases.description: Translate a desired RouterOS change into correct CLI paths, REST endpoints, and ordering before applying it.

use_cases.title: MikroTik Automation Support
use_cases.description: Build scripts or tools that call RouterOS REST APIs without assuming standard Linux or typical REST semantics.

use_cases.title: Configuration Review
use_cases.description: Check firewall, user, package, and device-mode workflows for RouterOS-specific risks and operational constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a RouterOS Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to add an IP address on RouterOS v7. Include the CLI command, REST endpoint, and common mistakes to avoid.

prompt_templates.title: Convert CLI to REST
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this RouterOS CLI workflow into REST calls for RouterOS v7. Explain the HTTP verb mapping and required identifiers.

prompt_templates.title: Review Firewall Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these RouterOS firewall changes for ordering issues, lockout risk, and RouterOS-specific syntax problems.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Automation Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RouterOS v7 automation workflow for package installation, device-mode checks, and rollback planning. Include REST timing risks.

output_examples.input: User asks how to update a RouterOS firewall rule through REST.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the firewall endpoint, explains that PATCH updates an existing rule, warns that rule order matters, and asks for the rule identifier before suggesting the final request.

output_examples.input: User asks why a Linux shell command fails on a MikroTik router.
output_examples.output: The response explains that RouterOS is not a general Linux userland, then offers the RouterOS CLI path that provides the closest equivalent result.

output_examples.input: User wants to enable a RouterOS package on CHR.
output_examples.output: The response distinguishes built-in packages from uploaded packages, explains why apply-changes is required, and notes the reboot impact before giving steps.

best_practices: Confirm the RouterOS major version before applying syntax or REST behavior.
Use lab routers or maintenance windows when testing commands that reboot or change access.
Replace example credentials, hosts, and HTTP URLs with production-safe access controls.
anti_patterns: Do not assume RouterOS supports Bash, coreutils, package managers, or Linux filesystem paths.
Do not apply REST POST actions without reviewing their device impact first.
Do not keep default admin or empty-password examples in production deployments.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands on my router?
faq.answer: No. It provides RouterOS guidance and examples. Your AI tool or workflow must perform any live connection separately.

faq.question: Which RouterOS versions are covered?
faq.answer: The skill targets RouterOS 7.x. RouterOS 6 may use different behavior and is outside the stated scope.

faq.question: Can it help with RouterOS REST API calls?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains RouterOS REST endpoints, verb mapping, identifiers, errors, and long-running command behavior.

faq.question: Can it replace MikroTik documentation?
faq.answer: No. It is a practical reference for AI assistance. Verify critical production changes against current vendor documentation.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The content is not malicious, but it covers router administration, default credentials, REST actions, and firewall changes.

faq.question: Can it help avoid Linux-specific mistakes?
faq.answer: Yes. It explicitly explains that RouterOS is not a GNU/Linux shell environment and gives RouterOS-specific alternatives.
